Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I'd study on certain days, but then I'd get distracted by other things going on in my life or just not feel like it. Most of my studying happened in random bursts, usually when I suddenly remembered something was due. I'm a visual learner, so I'd try to watch videos when I did study, but organizing a consistent schedule just never clicked for me this semester.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly spend a whole evening trying to catch up. Deadlines were pretty much the only thing that got me moving, and even then, I'd often start way too late. Other responsibilities and just general life stuff always seemed to take priority somehow.
Q3: Usually it was either panic about an upcoming deadline or getting an email notification about something. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything I'd missed, scroll around for a bit, and then leave without really doing much. It wasn't like I had a habit of checking it daily or anything—more like when guilt finally kicked in.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There was maybe a week or two early in the semester where I tried to make it a regular thing, but that faded fast. Life got busy, my motivation dropped, and logging in started to feel like a chore rather than something automatic. It never became a natural part of my day.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I learn better when I can see things explained visually. I watched a decent amount of those, actually. Being able to pause and rewind helped when I was confused. Other stuff like readings or discussion boards didn't really grab my attention the same way.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion boards entirely—I never posted anything. They felt awkward and like extra work that wouldn't really help me understand the material better. I also delayed a lot of the readings and smaller assignments because they didn't feel urgent enough until suddenly they were overdue.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to passing the course or understanding something I was confused about. If it seemed like busywork or wasn't going to show up on an exam, I'd push it aside. I needed to feel like there was an immediate payoff, otherwise my motivation just wasn't there.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist to me. If it wasn't required, I told myself I'd get to it later, and later never came. Required activities at least had some pressure attached, even if I still procrastinated on them. Optional things felt like extras I couldn't afford to spend energy on when I was already struggling with the basics.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download slides and PDFs to my laptop and look at them later without being online. Sometimes I'd screenshot parts of videos or take notes by hand while watching. I also used YouTube to find other explanations of concepts when the course materials weren't clicking for me.
Q10: There were times I'd spend an hour going through my downloaded notes or rewatching a video I'd saved, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also study with a friend occasionally, talking through concepts, but that obviously wouldn't appear in any log. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd open a lecture video once, download the slides, and then never go back to that page on the LMS. I'd review the slides on my own time, sometimes multiple times, but the system would only show that one initial access. It was just easier to have everything saved locally.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. I had stuff going on with my extracurriculars and some personal things that were stressing me out. Even though my stress levels weren't through the roof, I just felt disconnected from the course. The material started piling up and I couldn't find the energy to catch up.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired or had other responsibilities pulling at me, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Confusion played a role too—if I didn't understand something, I'd avoid it rather than push through. It was easier to just close the tab and deal with it later, except later kept getting pushed back.
Q14: Oh, for sure. I'd log in, click through some pages, maybe start a video, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd realize I'd been staring at the screen without absorbing anything. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actually learning. It happened more often than I'd like to admit.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was anxiety—starting felt overwhelming, so I'd avoid it. Part of it was just low motivation; I couldn't convince myself to care enough in the moment. By the time urgency hit, it was often too late to do a good job.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to do anything. I'd see a deadline approaching and finally force myself to log in and scramble. But even then, I'd sometimes miss them or submit incomplete work. Without a deadline looming, I had almost no drive to engage with the course materials.
Q17: Not really. I didn't get much feedback, and when I did, I was usually already behind on the next thing. It didn't change my behavior much because by the time feedback came, I'd mentally moved on or was too overwhelmed to adjust. Maybe if feedback had been more immediate, it would've helped.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty loose, which didn't help someone like me who needs external pressure. There was flexibility, but I just used that flexibility to procrastinate. If things had been more structured with stricter check-ins, I might have stayed on track better. The openness kind of worked against me.
Q19: Having videos was good for my learning style, so that made engagement easier when I actually sat down to watch them. But the lack of mandatory participation or regular quizzes made it easy to drift. Nothing was forcing me to stay engaged week to week, so I didn't. The design assumed more self-discipline than I had.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me—logging in, clicking around, watching some videos, but never actually completing the assignments. You can spend a lot of time in the LMS without producing anything. Maybe they're confused, overwhelmed, or just going through the motions without committing to the work.
Q21: That makes sense to me. If someone studies offline or uses other resources, their LMS activity would be low but they could still learn the material. I did some of that, but it didn't save me because I still didn't complete enough assignments. But yeah, low activity doesn't always mean low effort.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or understanding anything. They miss all the offline studying, the mental effort, the times you're struggling but not reaching out. It's just a surface-level picture of engagement.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in consistent effort. It's not just showing up or clicking through things—it's being mentally present and working toward understanding. I know I wasn't really engaged this semester, even when I was technically logged in.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how disconnected I felt or how much I was struggling with motivation. It wouldn't capture the times I studied offline or talked through concepts with friends. It also wouldn't show that I actually did care about learning, I just couldn't get myself to follow through consistently. There's a lot happening beneath the surface.
